1: ḥîḏôt — ‘hard questions’ (ESV), lit. riddles or enigmas. The root ḥûḏ produces ḥîḏāh: a dark, indirect saying requiring interpretive effort. The identical word is used for Samson’s riddle at the wedding feast (Judges 14:12–18) and in Numbers 12:8 where YHWH speaks with Moses ‘mouth to mouth, clearly, and not in riddles’ (wĕlōʾ bĕḥîḏōt). For Solomon to be tested by riddles frames wisdom as a contest requiring deep discernment, not surface knowledge. The queen travels to Jerusalem ‘to test him with riddles’ (lĕnassōt bĕḥîḏōt): nāsāh (Piel: to test, prove) is the same root as YHWH testing Abraham in Genesis 22:1. A human queen plays, in miniature, the role of divine examiner.
2: Solomon told her all her words (wayyaggêḏ-lāh šĕlōmōh ʾet-kol-dĕḇārêhā). Higgîd (Hiphil of nāgaḏ) = to declare, make known, tell. The emphasis is not that Solomon answered questions but that he told all her words back to her — she did not need to explain; wisdom anticipated her questions. ‘There was nothing hidden from Solomon that he did not tell her’ (lōʾ-nĕʿlam miššĕlōmōh dābār) — nāʿal (Niphal) = to be concealed. Wisdom as total transparency: nothing is hidden from the wise.
3: The queen saw (watteʾreʾ) the wisdom of Solomon (ʾet-ḥokmāt šĕlōmōh) and the house he had built. The combination of ḥokmāh (wisdom, abstract) and habbayit (the house, concrete) signals that in Chronicles, wisdom is not theoretical intelligence but the capacity that produces magnificent ordered reality — embodied most fully in the temple structure itself. The queen is a witness who sees wisdom’s material fruit.
4: ‘The food of his table, the seating of his servants, the attendance of his ministers’ (maʾăkal šulḥānô ûmôšaḇ ʿăḇādāyw ûmaʿămaḏ mĕšārĕtāyw). Three parallel noun phrases (maʾăkal / môšaḇ / maʿămaḏ) describe the ordered infrastructure of wisdom’s reign. maʿămaḏ (standing place, post) is used for the Levitical posts in the temple (cf. 2 Chronicles 35:2, 10, 15). The queen’s catalog ends with ‘his burnt offerings that he offered in the house of YHWH’ — the climax. Solomon’s administration culminates in worship, not statecraft.
5: ‘She said to the king: True was the word (ʾemet hāyāh haddāḇār) I heard in my land about your deeds and your wisdom.’ ʾemet (truth, faithfulness) is used as a predicate: the report is true. lōʾ heʾĕmantî lĕḏiḇrêhem — ‘I did not believe their words’ (heʾĕmîn = Hiphil of ʾāman, the root of ʾāmēn: to trust, believe). The queen’s confession is an epistemological moment: hearing was insufficient; seeing produced certainty. Reports (dĕḇārîm) about wisdom cannot substitute for wisdom’s presence.
6: ‘Half the greatness of your wisdom was not told me (lōʾ-huggadlî ḥăṣî rōḇ ḥokmātĕḵā).’ ḥăṣî = half; rōḇ = abundance, greatness. The formulation is superlative: even the full report she had received conveyed only half the reality. yāsaptā ʿal-haššĕmûʿāh = ‘you have surpassed the report’ (Hiphil of yāsap: to add/surpass). The queen’s speech is a formal testimony to wisdom exceeding reputation — a motif that the Chronicler uses to establish Solomon as the apex of human wisdom.
7: ʾašrê ʾănāšêḵā — ‘Blessed are your men!’ ʾašrê is the plural construct noun of happiness/blessedness — the same word that opens Psalms 1:1 (‘Blessed is the man’) and appears throughout the Psalter (Psalms 32:1-2; 2 Chronicles 33:12; etc.). The beatitude formula applied to Solomon’s servants: those who ‘stand before you continually and hear your wisdom’ (ʿōmĕḏîm lĕpānêḵā tāmîḏ wĕšōmĕʿîm ʾet-ḥokmātĕḵā). tāmîḏ (continually, the adverb of the daily offering) elevates their proximity from courtly privilege to liturgical status: they live in the daily rhythm of wisdom’s presence.
8: ‘Blessed be YHWH your God who delighted in you (ḥāpēṣ bĕḵā) to set you on his throne as king.’ ḥāpēṣ (Qal perfect: to delight in, take pleasure in) is the root of ḥēpeṣ (delight, pleasure; cf. Isaiah 53:10 ‘it pleased YHWH to crush him’). The queen articulates the Chronicler’s core theology: Solomonic kingship derives not from Davidic genetics alone but from divine delight. ‘Because your God loved Israel (ʾāhēḇ YHWH ʾet-yiśrāʾēl) to establish them forever (lĕhaʿămîḏô lĕʿōlām), he made you king over them to do justice and righteousness (mišpāṭ ûṣĕḏāqāh).’ The purpose of kingship is mišpāṭ (justice) and ṣĕḏāqāh (righteousness) — the two pillars of Davidic rule throughout the prophets (Isaiah 9:7; 2 Chronicles 11:4; Jeremiah 23:5).
9: The queen gives (wattitten) Solomon 120 talents of gold (mēʾāh wĕʿeśrîm kikkār zāhāḇ), spices (bĕśāmîm) in great abundance, and precious stones. kikkār (talent) = the standard large weight unit (c. 34 kg / 75 lbs). 120 talents of gold represents approximately 4,000 kg — an enormous diplomatic gift. bĕśāmîm (spices) — the same word used for the incense ingredients of the tabernacle (Exodus 25:6; 2 Chronicles 35:8). The queen’s gift vocabulary evokes the materials of worship.
10: The servants of Huram (ʿaḇḏê ḥûrām) and the servants of Solomon who brought gold from Ophir also brought ʿăṣê ʾalmugîm (algum wood) and precious stones. ʾalmugîm is a hapax (unique form) in Chronicles; the parallel in 1 Kings 10:11 reads ʾalgummîm — a metathesis (letter transposition) between the two traditions. The wood is likely sandalwood (Pterocarpus santalinus), used for temple musical instruments and the palace balustrade. Its rarity marks it as exotic tribute material.
11: Solomon made from the ʿăṣê ʾalmugîm terraces (mĕsillôt) for the house of YHWH and the king’s house, and lyres (kinnōrôt) and harps (nĕḇālîm) for the singers. mĕsillôt is a cognate of sōlal (to cast up a road/ramp) — probably a staircase balustrade or ramp inlaid with the exotic wood. The narrator comments: ‘none like them had been seen before in the land of Judah’ (wĕlōʾ nirʾû ḵēhem lĕpānîm bĕʾereṣ yĕhûḏāh) — the superlative uniqueness formula.
12: Solomon gave (nātan) the queen of Sheba all she desired (kol-ḥepṣāh). ḥēpeṣ (desire, pleasure) — the same root as ‘delighted’ in v8 (‘YHWH delighted in you’). What YHWH delights in becomes the standard of what the queen desires; the word link is probably intentional. ‘More than what she had brought to the king’ (mĕlĕḇad ʾăšer-hēḇîʾāh ʾel-hammelek) — Solomon gives back more than he receives, the hallmark of Solomonic abundance.
13: ‘The weight of gold (mišqal hazzāhāḇ) that came to Solomon in one year was 666 talents (šeš mēʾôt šiššîm wĕšēš kikkār).’ The number 666 in Chronicles is a fiscal calculation, not a symbolic one (contrast Revelation 13:18). As an annual income figure, 666 talents ≈ 22,700 kg of gold, representing the apex of ancient Near Eastern wealth. The accumulation of gold is presented as fulfillment of the covenant blessing (Deuteronomy 28:12).
14: ‘Besides what the merchants and traders brought’ (lĕḇaḏ mēʾandê hattāgārîm wĕharrōḵĕlîm mĕḇîʾîm). tāgārîm = merchants (from sāgar/Aramaic cognate, one who trades). rōḵĕlîm = traders, peddlers (cf. Ezekiel 27:13, 17, 22 for the same word in the Tyre trade oracle). ‘All the kings of Arabia (malkê ʿăraḇ) and governors of the land (paḥôt hāʾāreṣ)’ also brought tribute. paḥôt (plural of pehāh) = governor, a term that becomes common for Persian-period administrators; its appearance here in a Solomonic context may be anachronistic coloring or may reflect a genuine pre-exilic title.
15: Solomon made 200 large shields (māginnê zāhāḇ šāḥûṭ) of beaten gold. šāḥûṭ (Qal passive participle of šāḥaṭ) = hammered, beaten out; gold worked by hammering rather than casting. Each shield used 600 shekels of gold (šēš-mēʾôt zāhāḇ yāʿăleh ʿal-hammāgēn hāʾeḥāḏ). māgēn (round shield) vs. ṣinnāh (v16, the large rectangular body shield) — the two types of military shield represent different functional designs; their ceremonial gold versions were housed in the House of the Forest of Lebanon.
16: Three hundred small shields (šĕlōšet mēʾôt mēginnîm) of beaten gold; 300 shekels of gold each. ‘The king put them in the House of the Forest of Lebanon (bêt yaʿar hallĕḇānôn).’ The House of the Forest of Lebanon (1 Kings 7:2-5) was Solomon’s large armory/treasury building, named for its cedar pillars suggesting a cedar forest interior. Gold shields in an armory function as treasury reserves and royal display items simultaneously, converting military symbolism into wealth storage.
17: ‘The king made a great ivory throne (kissēʾ-šēn gāḏôl) and overlaid it with pure gold (wayyĕṣappēhû zāhāḇ ṭāhôr).’ kissēʾ (throne) is the seat of royal judgment. šēn (ivory, lit. tooth) = elephant tusk ivory. zāhāḇ ṭāhôr (pure gold) is the technical term for the highest-grade gold, identical to the term used for the most sacred temple furnishings (the ark’s overlay, the lampstand, the mercy seat — Exodus 25:11, 17, 31). The throne’s gold classification links Solomon’s judicial seat to the holy-of-holies furniture.
18: ‘The throne had six steps (šēš maʿălôt).’ maʿălôt = steps, ascents (same as the maʿălôt of Ps 120–134, the Psalms of Ascents). A golden footstool (kěḇeš zāhāḇ) was attached to the throne. The footstool of the king’s throne echoes the ark-as-footstool of YHWH (Psalms 99:5; 2 Chronicles 132:7; 1 Chronicles 28:2 where David calls the ark ‘the footstool of our God’). The throne geometry overlaps with temple geometry.
19: Twelve lions (šĕnêm ʿāśār ʾărāyôt) stood on the six steps, one on each side of each step. ʾaryēh (lion) is the royal animal of Judah (Genesis 49:9; Revelation 5:5). Twelve lions = the twelve tribes under royal-Judahite authority. The detail ‘nothing like it was made for any kingdom’ (lōʾ-nāʿăśāh kēn lĕḵol-mamlāḵāh) is the uniqueness formula applied to the entire construction, elevating Solomon’s throne beyond all human precedent.
20: ‘All the drinking vessels of King Solomon were gold (zāhāḇ), and all the vessels of the House of the Forest of Lebanon were pure gold; silver was not considered as anything (hakkesep lōʾ nẹhšāḇ) in the days of Solomon.’ nẹhšāḇ = counted, valued (Niphal of ḥāšaḇ: to reckon, compute). The statement is economic hyperbole communicating the superabundance of gold — silver has been demonetized by gold’s abundance.
21: The king’s ships (ʾŏniyyôt) went to Tarshish with the servants of Huram. Once every three years (lĕšālōš šānîm ʾaḥat) they returned carrying gold, silver, ivory (šēn), apes (qōpîm), and peacocks (tûkkiyyîm). Tarshish is generally identified with distant western Mediterranean ports (Tartessos in Spain?); ʾŏniyyôt taršîš may be a ship-type designation (ocean-going vessels) rather than exclusively ships going to Tarshish. tûkkiyyîm (peacocks) appears only here and in 1 Kings 10:22; possibly an Egyptian or Indian loanword. The exotic cargo list signals the reaches of Solomonic trade networks.
22: ‘King Solomon surpassed (waygaḏḏēl) all the kings of the earth in riches and wisdom.’ waygaḏḏēl (Hiphil of gāḏal: to make great, surpass) is the Chronicler’s greatness-ascription formula. The dual surpassing (wealth and wisdom) ties the two strands of the chapter together: the Queen of Sheba witnessed both, and the narrator confirms both.
23: ‘All the kings of the earth (kōl-malkê hāʾāreṣ) were seeking the face of Solomon (mĕḇaqqĕšîm ʾet-pĕnê šĕlōmōh) to hear his wisdom.’ bāqaš pĕnê (to seek the face of) is the idiom for seeking audience with a superior, frequently used of seeking YHWH’s face (Psalms 27:8; 2 Chronicles 7:14). Applied to Solomon, the phrase elevates him to a quasi-divine role: the nations who should seek YHWH’s face are directed toward Solomon as his wisdom’s embodiment. kōl-malkê hāʾāreṣ (all the kings of the earth) is the Chronicler’s hyperbole for universal recognition.
24: Each brought his tribute (minhātôw): silver and gold vessels, clothing (śalmôt), weapons (nešeq), spices (bĕśāmîm), horses, and mules. minhāh in this context = gift/tribute (not the technical sacrificial grain offering, though the word is shared). The tribute list parallels the materials of temple construction, suggesting that the nations’ tribute to Solomon implicitly supports the temple economy.
25: Solomon had 4,000 stalls for horses and chariots (ʾuryôt lassûsîm ûlammerkāḇôt) and 12,000 horsemen. ʾuryôt = stalls (for horses/donkeys); the root may relate to light or feeding place. Kings counts 40,000 stalls (1 Kings 4:26) — probably a scribal corruption in Kings; Chronicles’ 4,000 is consistent with the number of chariots deployed. The Chronicler placed them ‘in the chariot cities (ʿārê hāreḵeḇ) and with the king in Jerusalem.’
26: ‘He was ruling (mōšēl) over all the kings from the River (min-hannāhār) to the land of the Philistines and to the border of Egypt.’ hannāhār = the (Euphrates) River; no specification needed because the Euphrates is the river in OT geography. The formula describes the territory of the Abrahamic promise (Genesis 15:18: ‘from the river of Egypt to the great river, the Euphrates’). Solomon’s kingdom is presented as fulfilling the land promise.
27: ‘The king made silver as plentiful (wăyitten hammelek ʾet-hakkesep bîrûšālāyim kāʾăḇānîm) as stones in Jerusalem.’ The simile kāʾăḇānîm (like stones) compares silver to the most common, valueless substance in Judah’s landscape. Cedar wood is similarly compared to the common sycamore-fig trees (kaššiqmîm) of the Shephelah. The comparison inverts scarcity and abundance: rare materials become as common as ordinary ones under Solomonic reign.
28: Horses were imported for Solomon from Egypt and from all lands (mimmāṣrayim ûmikkol-hāʾărāṣôt). The horse trade was one of Solomon’s primary commercial activities (1 Kings 10:28-29). Deuteronomy 17:16 warns the king not to ‘multiply horses’ or return to Egypt for horses; the Chronicler reports the horse accumulation without the Deuteronomic critique that appears elsewhere. The tension between Deuteronomic law and Solomonic practice is left implicit.
29: ‘The rest of the acts of Solomon, from first to last (hāriʾšōnîm wĕhāʾaḥărōnîm), are they not written in the records of Nathan the prophet (ḏiḇrê nāṯān hannāḇîʾ), the prophecy of Ahijah the Shilonite (bĕniḇûʾāh ʾăḥiyyāh haššîlōnî), and in the visions of Iddo the seer (bĕḥăzôt yĕʿdô ḥōzeh)?’ Three prophetic sources are named. ḥōzeh (seer, from ḥāzāh: to see visions) is distinct from nāḇîʾ (prophet): the seer receives visual revelation while the prophet speaks the divine word. Chronicles frequently cites prophetic histories as sources, constructing a hermeneutic where all of history is prophetically witnessed.
30: Solomon reigned in Jerusalem over all Israel for 40 years (ʾarbaʿîm šānāh). Forty years is also David’s regnal length (1 Chronicles 29:27). The symmetry of father and son each reigning forty years frames the united monarchy as a perfectly balanced era. mālak (to reign, be king) is the regnal summary verb; its use here in the Qal perfect marks the completion of Solomon’s reign as a narrative unit.
31: ‘Solomon slept with his fathers (wayyiškab šĕlōmōh ʿim-ʾăḇōtāyw) and was buried in the city of his father David (bĕʿîr dāwîḏ ʾāḇîw).’ The royal death formula wayyiškab ʿim-ʾăḇōtāyw is the standard regnal conclusion. The burial in ‘the city of his father David’ anchors Solomon in the Davidic dynastic tradition. Rehoboam his son reigned in his place (wayyimlōḵ rĕḥaḇʿām bĕnô taḥtāyw) — the succession formula, setting up 2 Chronicles 10’s crisis immediately.
